name: swiftui-ui-patterns
description: Best practices and example-driven guidance for building SwiftUI views and components. Use when creating or refactoring SwiftUI UI, designing tab architecture with TabView, composing screens, or needing component-specific patterns and examples.
capabilities: Provide patterns for TabView architecture with per-tab navigation stacks
Show sheet routing with enum-driven centralized sheet presentation
Demonstrate form and settings patterns with proper focus management
Guide on grids, lists, and scroll views for efficient layouts
Explain deep link routing and programmatic navigation
Show theming, haptics, and media handling patterns
author: Dimillian
securitySummary: Pure documentation skill containing only Markdown files with SwiftUI best practices and code examples. No executable code, scripts, network calls, file system access, or command execution capabilities. All 498 static findings are false positives from markdown documentation being misinterpreted by pattern-based scanning.
valueStatement: Creating SwiftUI views requires understanding navigation architecture, component patterns, and state management. This skill provides ready-to-use patterns for tabs, sheets, lists, grids, and more so you can ship faster with fewer bugs.
userTitle: Build SwiftUI Views with Best Patterns
limitations: Does not generate complete SwiftUI project files
Does not provide backend API integration code
Does not include unit tests or test utilities
Does not cover SwiftUI accessibility beyond basic labels

useCases.title: Architect Tab Navigation
useCases.description: Set up scalable TabView with per-tab NavigationStack and route enums for clean navigation state.

useCases.title: Implement Sheets Properly
useCases.description: Replace prop-drilling sheet bindings with centralized enum-driven sheet routing pattern.

useCases.title: Refactor Legacy Views
useCases.description: Modernize older SwiftUI code using modern state primitives like @Observable and @Environment.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Tab Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to set up a TabView with multiple tabs that each have their own NavigationStack and independent navigation history.

promptTemplates.title: Sheet Routing
promptTemplates.prompt: How do I create a centralized sheet routing pattern using an enum so any view can present modals without passing bindings through many layers?

promptTemplates.title: List Performance
promptTemplates.prompt: What are the best patterns for building efficient Lists and ScrollViews with pull-to-refresh, scroll-to-top, and proper row reuse?

promptTemplates.title: Form Validation
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to implement form validation with proper focus state management, keyboard handling, and accessibility in SwiftUI.

bestPractices: Keep views small and focused on a single responsibility
Use @Environment for shared dependencies instead of prop-drilling
Prefer @Observable and modern state over older view models
Centralize navigation state in a router object per tab
antiPatterns: Avoid mixing sheet(isPresented:) and sheet(item:) for the same concern
Do not nest @Observable objects inside other @Observable objects
Avoid storing view instances in navigation paths; use lightweight route data
Do not use raw Color values throughout views; use semantic theme colors

faq.question: Which SwiftUI versions are supported?
faq.answer: Patterns target iOS 17+ and macOS 14+ with some iOS 18+ specific APIs noted.

faq.question: How many items can Lists handle efficiently?
faq.answer: Use List for long content with built-in row reuse. For custom layouts prefer ScrollView + LazyVStack.

faq.question: Can I use these patterns with third-party libraries?
faq.answer: Patterns are framework-agnostic and integrate with any SwiftUI-compatible navigation solution.

faq.question: Is my data safe when using these patterns?
faq.answer: These are view-layer patterns with no network or storage access. Data safety depends on your implementation.

faq.question: Why does my sheet not dismiss properly?
faq.answer: Use .sheet(item:) instead of .sheet(isPresented:) when state represents a selected model. Avoid if let inside sheet body.

faq.question: How do these patterns compare to UIKit?
faq.answer: SwiftUI patterns favor declarative state over imperative updates. NavigationStack replaces UINavigationController.
